15. Fundamental Equations. -- We have seen that matter may be considered as an aggregate of atoms. It may, therefore, be considered as under the influence of two or more sets of forces. We have defined two sets of forces to be those which act upon an atom, and those which act upon a molecule.

16. Law of the Conservation of Energy. -- The first statement of the principle of the conservation of energy is, therefore, that all the energy which a material system possesses it can derive from and can part with to external bodies.

This is the first law of energy. It is not difficult to see the reason for it.

At any moment there are two sets of forces acting upon a material system, -- one acting upon every atom in the system, the other upon every molecule. The latter forces are called the kinetic forces.

In the chapter on the Conservation of Energy, we saw that the sum of the kinetic energy in a system of two kinds was constant. We have now seen how this is brought about. The kinetic energy in a system of two kinds is called the potential energy.

17. Kinetic Energy of a Material System. -- The energy of a system, consisting of two kinds of material, may be considered as made up of two parts, the energy of the

MATTER AND ITS PROPERTIES 15

The total energy of a system may be divided into two parts, one representing the energy of the system, the other representing the energy of motion of its parts. The former, the free energy, is called the kinetic energy of the system, the latter the potential energy.
